speaker: interviewer
text: And you weren't chosen, but the group across from you was chosen for the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was the Russian gypsies, like I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all in the barracks by themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all in the bar-- yeah. Whole barrack was full with Russian gypsies, but we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't know what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know it, no. No, we didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, didn't know what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were Russian gypsies over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just prisoners of some kind, you know, but nobody really knew. But they were all Russian gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], the rumors went. And we heard them screaming up to the things. And of course, the men knew what was going on. And there was somewhere they had-- it comes out, you know? The rumors go around, that they emptied that block last night. And, and now, on the other side from us, on the other side-- don't forget we were separated. But the camps, different camps were separated with wires-- that's electric wire. So on the other side was men, sick block, just for Jewish men. Jewish men. Now, they had, again, a week or so later, they had the, called that appel, [SPEAKING GERMAN] appel, [SPEAKING GERMAN], and they go all in again. And again, hear those trucks going. Now, as I told you, that's how I was going to tell you about those [? clappers ?] on the top, on those little windows where you peek out there. And young girls, we were dying because we couldn't look out the front. You can't look out in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we looked out to the thing. And over the wire, you could see it on the other side. You could look a little, over there, you see the trucks were all aligning.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see those trucks. And you see them filling up the, the trucks with the fellas coming out, with the little, like you see them in some of those pictures here. You see them coming out bones sticking out, and this and that. And [? they ?] had to go. Some of them couldn't even make it up on those trucks. And they nasty and kicking, all this and that, make them all practically crawl. And some of them begged. Oh, herr officer, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I'm ready to go to work. I could work. And filled up that, and emptied that whole sick block out over there. The next day, they were all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hear that screaming going on, because they were driving out, going [? all ?] the way to crematoriums, [? around ?] [? the end to that. ?] So this was not the experience, as far as the emptying is concerned, and the crying, you know, the screaming. And, and every time they [INAUDIBLE] it was an appel block that they hauled up, get off the streets. We had to [? get off. ?] There was always something happening-- something not good, OK? What kind of interaction did you have with the other prisoners? Did you make any friendships?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. Every-- I mean, I have made friends, friendship with them. And even some of them, we had communicated afterwards. But you know, a lot of them came home sick. A lot of them died right then and there, too. A lot of them died in the camp. So you have-- you know, if you don't get along with your personal friend, foul, fellow, friend, then, then you have no one. Then you are alone. Can't get along with the Nazis. You can't get along with the, with some of the other helps there. So you have to get along.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can't have any squealing going on, and anything like that. You can't have that. You know what I mean? There's a lot of little things which you do and was said, and, and you don't want the authorities. You know what I mean? So it's got to be all-- so there wasn't there, that people squealed on each other. We just all lived there for one thing, for to survive, for survival. [? When, ?] maybe not, maybe yes. It wasn't questioned. Did the friendships cross lines? Were you mostly friends with the other gypsies? Or was it friends with the Jews or the political prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, anybody. Like I told you, this, this lady up there, I've, I've-- I've-- I've a nice woman. And I felt real bad about that with her sons, you know? I figure, I'm young. Here, she's 64 years old, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what a thing to do to this woman. And of course, we talked about it. You don't have nothing else to do than talk, you know? And you're laying there. Nine o'clock, the lights go out. You know what I mean? You talk, and I'm not even allowed. So that's all you do is talk about things, about your own experience in life, about her, and about his, and about this, and about that. Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any difference, do you think, in the way that the political prisoners were treated, or the gypsies were treated, or the Jews were treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This is what I said before. The end is the same. I don't care who they were, and who-- we all went through the same thing. We all with this stuff. And we all had the diseases. We all had this. [? Now, ?] some of them were in the kitchen. Some of them played in, in the orchestra, some of them. Everybody had something. Some of them never did anything, just died right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them could never get out of those sick blocks. Like, for instance, my sisters, my little brother. Some of them couldn't make it, you know? So-- When did you find out about, when did you find out about the gassings and the crematoria? Was that right after you arrived, or were you sheltered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, through-- our dad told us about that. And of, course, you could-- now, when you were made aware of the situation and you could be looking around you. Because we had smoke stacks going 24 hours a day. There was just black smoke coming out for 24 hours a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They told us. My dad told us, you know, and other people, too. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're in-- you're living with 500 people in one block, let me tell you something. It's like putting-- visualize yourself, can you visualize 500 people in so much square foot there? With the open toilets in the back there, with the lye smell, and the whole schmear? And the people lying dead, piling up in the back? Do you know what odor is in this, those things? And, you know, you don't even notice those odors anymore after a while. You adapt.

speaker: interviewee
text: The human people adapt. You adapt. I don't mean you survive it, but you adapt. You have to, otherwise you're finished right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us, he said, that is where the, that's where the gas chambers are. And he said, and see that smokes out there? That's where the bodies are burned. Now, he was trying to tell us that there was some kind of a conveyor system. I've never seen it. But he said that in the gas thing, that they were opened up because they had so many-- so many were dying there, they couldn't have handled them all, [INAUDIBLE]. So they had kind of like a conveyor system in there, I imagine. And that they got rid of so many bodies a day, and they had to. I don't think they could shovel them, every one of them up there. I don't know how many thousand they had a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see the conveyor system?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'll tell you, I dreamt about it many times, later in life. When I was out of the concentration camp, I used to dream that I seen this conveyor going. And there was little men on that conveyor. And I used to go half up to it, and then I slid off it again. It's a terrible dream. But you know, lately, I haven't had it. I haven't had it for years. But I had it for a long time, that conveyor, that screaming going on up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you actually see it, or was it just from your dad talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. No, we see after. We see it. Then you get, you know, when you start going on the outside, working. Aussenkommandos, you work around crematoriums. You [INAUDIBLE], there is nothing. And you don't really pay that much attention to it because you're busy getting that day going, doing your work, and trying to get not in trouble with those Nazis. You do your thing. Pain in the feet, of [INAUDIBLE]. It were raining cats and dogs, the water dripping down. No underwear. Sometimes in the beginning, we had our period-- pardon the expression-- we had our periods. We didn't get anymore, later on in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long later, did it stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I, I probably had it, you know, that's because of the food. The whole thing changed there. I probably had it for about five or six months, and then none no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you supplies for that, when you had it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You just bled?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a nice thing to say. It's not a nice thing to say at all.

words.text: nice thing to say at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You  can tell me anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was under-- we had, like, they gave you, like, an undergarment. They did give you pants, too. But, you know, people, they stole little things in there. You lost things.

speaker: interviewer
text: For desperation, it's not really stealing because you're desperate. People are desperate, OK? So sometimes you lost your underwear if you didn't lay on it at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when you want to rinse it out. I used to rinse my stuff out a little bit, with sand, in the wash bin, long thing. And then I'd wash them out a little bit. Then I rolled it in my dress, the damp, and I put it underneath where I laid. Then it was dry in the morning when I got up, before I had to go out for work. Because that's the only way you could survive a little bit for your cleanliness. That don't mean you didn't get the dry [? rot. ?] I had it, too, but I-- that's how we did it. You learned little tricks for the survival there. If something was a little damp yet, but was a little bit clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you had your period, you just bled? It was just a mess?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, took the underwear, that slip, and we ripped it. We made little rags, and guarded those little rags like they were gold, those little rags.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rinsed them out a little bit in that sand thing, and put them under the mattress and dried them. Then nobody else steal the little rags from you. Because you couldn't even find rags, because you'd need them for, for looking for your feet, you know what I mean? Rags was a very rare thing there, too. So we used each other, little goodies, that way. And of course, you had to be really-- for survival purposes, if you didn't tie your little bowl around your skirt, you lost your bowl and you don't eat. Was-- there was both men and women in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all mixed. In the beginning, we were mixed. Now remember, later on, about 18 months later, we got separated. Now, of course, my mother was already dead. My dad was already dead. My sister, Mathilda, was already dead. We didn't know what happened to William, because we never found, never heard from him at that time. But we thought he was in one of the camps. That he just--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he disappear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the beginning. Right in the beginning. He was work-- he got the numbers with us and everything. And then, oh, about a week or so, him and my brother, Louie. But my brother, Louie said, told us, he was not with him. They shipped Louie to-- where did I told you the cards were from?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau. And William, they, they said something about Ravensbruck. But Ravensbruck was a woman's prison at that time, mostly woman in there. But we never knew where he went. There's no papers from him, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was the brother that went to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, my brother Louie?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Louie, at that time, he was, he was the service. That's the one that's in the service. He was 23. Because my sister Mathilda was about 21. He was about 23. And he-- [INAUDIBLE] [? said he ?] survived. We never got to see him no more. Because our parents, that's one thing that dad told us. Should we ever, any one of  us survive, if you girls, you boys, anybody survives here, go back to the [GERMAN] house. If the house isn't there, go to the spot. That's where we all meet. That was the thing. That was, we had that in our head, and that's exactly what we did. Took us, some of us, three weeks, three months to get back, because there was no transportation. All depends where we wound up on the end, in those different camps. We were separated. And the war was finished. And there was no way in the world that you got any ride from anybody. Maybe the soldier, the American soldiers. We happened-- I happened to get free, our camp. We were in Schlieben. That's near the [GERMAN] there. They were freed with the-- we were freed by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you were not in Auschwitz for the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end? No. But four months. You see, they already-- the Nazis already knew about this, that's what we found out later. We didn't know it that the war was getting towards the end and they were going to clean up that mess. They didn't want anybody to see what they were going through, but they did that. But they didn't-- they didn't have time to do it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they started to clean out some of them. They just called an appel and they-- you, austreten. You, austreten. Now, they didn't tell you where you're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Austreten is what? Go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on out from the line. Stay out, come on out. Out. Doesn't mean austreten [INAUDIBLE]. Austreten from the line, OK? And that's when they take you, and you march. You follow. You just follow those people. There's nothing you can do. Otherwise, they shoot you right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who from your family went out with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went all by myself. My sister, Linda, Lena, the one who I told you was home for three weeks, who died? She got with my sister, Louisa. She had my brother, Ernst. She had my brother, Otto and my brother, Karl. She had all the little ones and her, and they went to Bergen-Belsen. They shipped her to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to Schlieben there, out the door now. What was it? Louis went to Dachau. So Louis went to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, [? Plime, ?] went to Ravensbruck. She got freed from there. And that's how we were spread. Because the parents were already dead. The other two died there, too, [? and some ?] [? other. ?] So then two weeks, I wasn't there when the other died, because my sister, Lena, told us that. That, oh, she said, I'm so glad we're home. She was so sick. She was only just a skeleton, that she even made that. She used to pass out on the way. And the kids are small. And the kids get scared and they run away from her about a mile down the road, and she go into, like, convulsions. And the rain, sometimes, would wake her up and then she'd catch up with them. And that's how she made it. But she wanted to just bring those kids home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about, when [INAUDIBLE], because my oldest sister was married there. She got in touch with her. That's when-- and then, of course, she got, there was no telephones. We had no telephones. It had to be all done with the wagons. There was no cars either, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother-in-law came and said that she was down there with the kids and this and that. And she kept her down there for awhile, and then they brought her. And she was, at that point, was dead. Died once she was there. There was not-- the American authorities said there was not a organ in her body what was not infected. And what she did is another thing. When got out, they went to the farms. The farmers gave them food. And, you know, when you're so sick and so run down, you have to get hospitalized. You got to get baby food to get your stomach going, otherwise you'll die. And that infect everything. Nothing stuck with her anymore. She was all gone. She was 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful, beautiful girl. Sister, Lena. You got her name there. Beautiful, beautiful, beautiful girl. And she said, I'm glad I'm home.

speaker: interviewer
text: She laid up in the bed where my parents [INAUDIBLE] house, you know? She said, I'm so happy. And there was a little sunshine. She was real happy. And she wanted apples and stuff. And they-- we didn't want to give her anything, you know? But the doctors said, give it to her. It's not going to save her from nothing. So that's what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's eating her apple, and we're all standing around, crying. So sadness. Sadness.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if my voice goes on that thing, it kind of goes out a little bit. But I can't help it. So it's, it's, it was a terrible thing. Now, where were we again? So you were leaving Auschwitz and going to the second camp. Did you march there? Did they take you on a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were back in the wagon, in the-- in the-- in the-- what do you call those wagons?

speaker: interviewer
text: The truck or wagon? Cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cattle cars again, yeah. And we didn't know where we were going. We didn't know where we were going at all. And we were there overnight. I say, we were about one day and one night on the train from Auschwitz to Schlieben. That's next to the [INAUDIBLE] down there somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were we going? What's going to happen to us? Where are they going to put us? But we also knew that as the rumors went through the years, there was not a-- the worst camp is where we were. And wherever we were going to go, it's supposed to be, supposed to be a little better than what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What month and year? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I was in Schlieben for about four months. And we got freed in '45, in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was maybe January?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a good four months. Let it be five months. I know, let it be five-- four or five months. It was not long. Because it was really coming towards the end. And there was an underground munition factory. And you wouldn't believe it. Lining up again, the troop what just came in, commander walked through. You, out. You, out. You, out. I was picked, again, for the kitchen. I couldn't believe it. Here I was again. And I was-- but I looked a little better, I guess, than the rest of them. Here, peel the potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have to go to munition factory. I figured it would be better for me. Now, I get going there again. I'm working in the kitchen, peeling potatoes, and the Nazis eating out there. And if you got the guard, you know, when you eat, you don't like it, then you put it back in the plate, chew a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, all that stuff was vitamin stuff. It was better than what they were cooking there. So what we did, we took all that garbage. First was, we ate, ourselves, first. Now, you're in the block there with hundreds of girls and they're working in that munition factory. They're all yellow in the eyes, and they're-- OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you throw that under, for the pigs, whatever they were-- they were giving that to the, feeding the swine with that, the pigs. So what we used to do, we used to take bundles. And we used to take it in our slips and tie it. All mixed up, whatever it was, doesn't matter. And those girls were just waiting for us to come. Because we got marched in the morning, out. There was about 20 of us, marched out. And in the evening, marched in again.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to the kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always under guard. Yeah. So if they ever found you with that little thing, you would've been in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those-- that's-- that's-- that's how you get strong. In one way, you do get strong because you can't do it, OK? It's like God coming to fill a little garbage there, OK? You're all, that's the way it is. Was it all women in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Schlieben now, that other camp, where I was. And now, it was a-- they had, already, all old guards, old men in there. They were 60-some years old, old guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was in the magazine there, making like, what do you call that when you count every year? You count what's left over in there? Inventory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inventory. So I was elected inventory. And I'm counting all the stock what they had in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an old man, old German SS man. And just to have a little conversation, he didn't look so mean, you know? And I said, well, how are the things going out there, I said. Are you-- no, he was from the big city, you know? He says, no, my wife is dead. We were bombed. So he was a little talkative, the man. And as I, second day, I had a little confidence. I said, gosh, maybe I should take one of those [INAUDIBLE] meat. It was like dog food in the cans, you know? I figured I'll kind of take one of those and bring it to the girls in the block. But I didn't touch nothing till toward the end, towards just before we got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And remember, we got those striped things on. And we had a, like a [? cord, ?] like a [? coddle ?] type of a thing, because you always had our bowls on this, you know, and a spoon. So you had that little support there. But what I did is, I took a can and I stuck it under my arm. Even here you couldn't see it, you know, but I kept it over here. And, of course, you've got to march. [INAUDIBLE], and those are girls. This is a barrack of girls. And they walk to you up to the left and go up the stairs. You go in your barracks. And they watch you almost going down the hall, all the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured, oh god, I'm doing good. And I was just going to go open my door to get into that thing there, and halt! So I turned around. I figured what the hell? It can't be that. Must have seen, must have seen something there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Number [SPEAKING GERMAN], halt! She called a number out. Was just a little thing-- devil in the self. And walked right up to me and put her hand right in there. I couldn't believe it. She's right in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she says, where did they-- where did that come from? I said, well, I was-- I had to tell her, I was working the magazine for the last couple of days and I wanted to bring it for the girls in the block. So she wrote down what I said, put my number, and told me [NON-ENGLISH]. I could go in the room. So I figured, oh boy. So all the girls, you know, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Now, there was some French ladies in there, elderly women. They said, now, don't you dare change your mind. Just tell them that you're not finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not going to let you get away with that. They're going to come back. You wait and see.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't change your mind. Don't lie. Just tell them the truth, because what else you going to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not too long, she was right. The commander, the commander, that little thing there, called me out. I had to come out, march. Well, then he asked me about the, about the, where I got this, and I told him the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he said, are you sure? Are you sure that officer, that soldier-- he thought that old soldier gave that to me. See, they didn't even trust each other anymore, you see? It was already going towards the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no sir. I said, I did that on my own. I said, I certainly would tell you that. I said, if that would help my situation, I would tell you. It would be easy for me to say, but it is not so. Well, I had to march with them out in the big main office out in front. Oh, you know, with soldiers like [INAUDIBLE] soldiers, when they should lay in the hand grenades, and boxes, and all that stuff, office tables, whatever they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I said, you want to [? tell us, ?] change your mind? Isn't that it? And I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's the guy who had given me that job when I first came, picked me for that job. I wasn't too worried about nothing. I didn't know. I figured, what the heck, they could only kill me. But if they don't kill me, I got to go out to that munition factory. That's why I was worried. Because I figured, that's the end now. My job's gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with them. And I said, no officer, herr commandant, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They would like that to say it any different, but the old man did not. That man, that officer, did not. He had nothing to do with it. He don't know nothing about it. Well, he said, well, we will see. So he said, he gave some kind of order to the, the officer there and some military talk, you know what I mean? And before you know it, that [INAUDIBLE] told me to step on that. There's a double hand grenade box there. It was, like, milk boxes, the old-fashioned wooden boxes, and they had their hand grenades, and there was two of them. So I had to step on that and put my hands in the back. And there was somehow they would hook, they hooked me on that with the cord, and I'm staying on the box. I said, what the heck are they going to do to me now? This is stupid. They're going to explode me here? They won't do that, because they're going to explode them self. So right after that, they took one box off. So now I'm hanging there a little bit, but I'm still touching a little bit. Now they took off the other box. Yeah, I don't think you ever hung on your arms, did you? You're not on the floor, and you're not on the, on the end. You know, you're hanging. You're not really, but you're not on the [INAUDIBLE]. So I don't know how long I hung there. Quite a while. Oh, terrible. Hours?

words.text: the heck are they going to

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. So I finally-- when I hadn't came to again, you know what I mean? So they finally came and took me down. And almost like there was something wrong here, with my, my shoulders. And they took, brought me back to the barracks. The next-- I thought now I was through with them. Now, the next night, when this appel, that the whole camp has to come out-- Block 5, Block 6, Block 8, whatever their numbers were there. And every block kapo has to count the people and then give-- call in the report. And they'd march by there and you'd give them the reports. And after the report is done, if there's any kind of a remarks to be made, the commander does it right then and there. Now, what they were doing, they were bringing one of those square tables, just a square pit, just a ordinary square bench, a work, a little work bench, where men, men work at, but a little lower. And it, it's, it's crossed like this on each side. There's enough room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, they called my number out. I said, oh, god. What the heck's go-- what they going to do to me now? So in front of all those people, all the camp-- there was a reason for that, them doing that in front of all those people-- How many people? Thousands?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the whole camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh god, because they speak to the micro-- to the microphone, you know what I mean, to the monster. The whole camp, then, I don't remember. There was lots of them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had to walk there. OK? So I came from that thing, and the two Nazis there told me to get close to the bench and put my, from my waist, down. OK? And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said, put your feet into this. Now remember, there's a board there. Your feet into this, and you're bending over. And they take your hand, and you're tied. Now, you ain't going nowhere. I figure, what they going to do to me? Then they call-- he called out, [GERMAN]. I was supposed to get from the one or two, one, two, three, four. 150 is [GERMAN]. I stole the food from the kitchen. And as [? a solid ?] sign, that don't anybody ever get the idea that you could do this. So I think I got about-- I should say, I got the hole here to prove this. They told me, maybe about 25 or 30. Don't forget, they had that--

speaker: interviewer
text: They whipped you? They whipped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had that short one. I had one. I wish I kept it, but I was afraid of that thing. And they had, it was real-- that-- that-- that SS thing, was with leather wrap. And in that, I don't know if your husband ever seen anything like that. It's round. There was a lead thing in there. Well, I never paid any attention to it till this happened. That's what hurts though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were doing one, two. And they doing it fast, because 150's quite a bit. So I think they said 25, and I passed out. I don't know. I didn't count anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at 75, they had to stop because the blood was splashing through the dress there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
